title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a student called Adam talking about volunteering on a heritage railway. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a student called Adam talking about volunteering on a heritage railway. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A student called Adam talks about volunteering on a heritage railway.
extracts.audio_script: Hi. I’m Adam, and last year I spent several weekends volunteering at Brookfield Heritage Railway, which operates old trains along a short route through the countryside. I had visited as a child, but I only considered volunteering when I needed a practical project for my [9]history course[/9] at college. Some classmates chose museums, while I wanted to learn about a place still using original machinery and buildings. The railway is run largely by volunteers, with qualified staff responsible for engines and passenger safety.||I had expected the most interesting object to be one of the old trains. They are certainly impressive, but the place I enjoyed seeing most was the restored [10]signal cabin[/10], with its long row of coloured handles and windows overlooking the track. A volunteer explained how signals were controlled before computers were used. On my first day I did not work close to trains. All beginners attend an induction and are issued with [11]protective glasses[/11] before entering workshop areas, even if their task seems unlikely to produce dust or small pieces of metal. The railway takes safety very seriously.||Volunteers do a wide range of jobs. People with engineering experience assist with repairs, while others care for gardens beside the platforms or welcome visitors. At first, I worked in the [12]ticket office[/12], answering basic questions and handing passengers their travel information. It was a good way to become familiar with the timetable. Later I helped organise an exhibition. We discovered boxes of old [13]railway photographs[/13] showing staff, platforms and passengers from over sixty years ago. Rather than keeping them in storage, we selected some to display, with captions based on local people's memories.||During school holidays, the railway offers activities for visitors. Children's rides are popular, and there is usually a small model train display. This year the event attracting the largest number of advance bookings is a [14]guided tour[/14] of areas not normally open to the public, including the engine shed and signal cabin. Volunteers who want more practical experience can attend specialist sessions. I was most interested in the [15]carpentry workshop[/15], where we learned how wooden seats and signs are repaired using traditional methods. It made me appreciate the skills needed to maintain even small details of the railway.||After I had helped for twelve weekends, the manager presented me with a [16]volunteer award[/16] at a small meeting. I had not expected anything formal, and it has been useful to mention on applications. Visitors often ask where they can get refreshments. There used to be only a snack cart on busy days, but this summer a new [17]station café[/17] will open in a former waiting room. Its profits will help fund repairs. The next major aim is to return a damaged [18]steam engine[/18] to working condition. This will require a lot of money and expert time, so the railway is holding several fundraising events. I am planning to continue helping, and I would recommend the experience to anyone interested in local history.

dialogue.speaker: adam
dialogue.text: Hi. I’m Adam, and last year I spent several weekends volunteering at Brookfield Heritage Railway, which operates old trains along a short route through the countryside. I had visited as a child, but I only considered volunteering when I needed a practical project for my history course at college. Some classmates chose museums, while I wanted to learn about a place still using original machinery and buildings. The railway is run largely by volunteers, with qualified staff responsible for engines and passenger safety.||I had expected the most interesting object to be one of the old trains. They are certainly impressive, but the place I enjoyed seeing most was the restored signal cabin, with its long row of coloured handles and windows overlooking the track. A volunteer explained how signals were controlled before computers were used. On my first day I did not work close to trains. All beginners attend an induction and are issued with protective glasses before entering workshop areas, even if their task seems unlikely to produce dust or small pieces of metal. The railway takes safety very seriously.||Volunteers do a wide range of jobs. People with engineering experience assist with repairs, while others care for gardens beside the platforms or welcome visitors. At first, I worked in the ticket office, answering basic questions and handing passengers their travel information. It was a good way to become familiar with the timetable. Later I helped organise an exhibition. We discovered boxes of old railway photographs showing staff, platforms and passengers from over sixty years ago. Rather than keeping them in storage, we selected some to display, with captions based on local people's memories.||During school holidays, the railway offers activities for visitors. Children's rides are popular, and there is usually a small model train display. This year the event attracting the largest number of advance bookings is a guided tour of areas not normally open to the public, including the engine shed and signal cabin. Volunteers who want more practical experience can attend specialist sessions. I was most interested in the carpentry workshop, where we learned how wooden seats and signs are repaired using traditional methods. It made me appreciate the skills needed to maintain even small details of the railway.||After I had helped for twelve weekends, the manager presented me with a volunteer award at a small meeting. I had not expected anything formal, and it has been useful to mention on applications. Visitors often ask where they can get refreshments. There used to be only a snack cart on busy days, but this summer a new station café will open in a former waiting room. Its profits will help fund repairs. The next major aim is to return a damaged steam engine to working condition. This will require a lot of money and expert time, so the railway is holding several fundraising events. I am planning to continue helping, and I would recommend the experience to anyone interested in local history.
